hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: How Much and How Many? -- Talking About Patients, Data and Market Access
lesson.menu_desc: Fechando o bloco 6-10: como falar dos números que importam -- quantos pacientes, quanta evidência, quanto acesso -- e como o acesso mudou (o que used to ser e o que é hoje) -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- How Much and How Many?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Lesson 10 -- How Much and How Many?

listenings.text: Emmanuele, Marc Dubois here. Before the business review on Thursday, I want you ready for three data questions, because Global always asks them. First: how many patients can take this treatment in Brazil? I want the patient population, more or less. Second: how much access is there today, in the public sector and the private sector? And third: how much evidence do we have for the new dose? Do not give me a hundred numbers. Give me a few numbers that matter, and one sentence for each. I remember two years ago there used to be almost no access in the public sector. I want to know what changed. Talk to you Thursday.

Good morning, everyone. Claire Bennett, with the market access update from the global immunology team. First, the good news. The patient population for the dermatology treatment is large, and it is growing in several countries. There is a lot of new evidence for the higher dose, and funding in the private sector is stable. Now the difficult part. There used to be very little access in the public sector, and today there is still not much. We have a few programs under review, but the reimbursement decision is slow. So, before Thursday, please send me two numbers: how many patients your team is treating this quarter, and how much public funding you have secured. Thank you, everyone.

extra_audio.text: Good morning, Marc. Here is the market access update for Brazil. The patient population is large, and it is growing. We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose. But there is not much access in the public sector yet, and funding is slow. Two years ago there used to be almost none, so this is real progress.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the money that pays for a program or a treatment
all the people who have a disease and can take a treatment
the amount of a medicine a patient takes at one time
the medical care and medicine a doctor gives to a patient
how much a health plan or the public system pays for a treatment
facts and studies that show a treatment works
the numbers and facts about results

paras: Two years ago, a patient with a serious skin disease in the public sector in Brazil used to wait a long time. There used to be very little access to modern treatment, almost no public funding, and not much evidence in Portuguese for the doctors. Many patients used to pay for everything alone, and many simply stopped.

Today the picture is different, and the numbers tell the story. The patient population is large, and it is growing. There is a lot of new evidence for the higher dose, and several new programs give better coverage in the private sector. There are still a few problems: in the public sector there is not much funding yet, and the reimbursement decision is slow. But a manager who reports data well does not hide the difficult part.

So how do you talk about all of this in a global meeting? You separate two families of words. For the things you can count one by one -- patients, doctors, programs, centers -- you ask how many, and you say a few or several. For the things you cannot count -- data, evidence, access, funding, coverage -- you ask how much, and you say a little. And when you compare the past with today, one small chunk does the work: it used to be like this; now it is different.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Access to treatment in Brazil is exactly the same today as it was two years ago.
Access used to be very limited, and today it is better in several ways -- and you describe it with how many / how much and used to.
A manager should only report the good numbers and hide the problems from the global team.

items: Two years ago there used to be a lot of public funding for the treatment.
The opposite: "almost no public funding" and "very little access" two years ago.
Today the patient population is large and growing.
The second paragraph says exactly this: "The patient population is large, and it is growing."
You use how many for things you can count, and how much for things you cannot count.
The last paragraph explains the two families: patients (count) take how many; data, evidence, funding (no count) take how much.
The text says a good manager hides the difficult part of the data.
It says the opposite: "a manager who reports data well does not hide the difficult part."

parts: be very little access in the public sector. Today, "
patients can take the treatment?" -- the population is large. "
evidence do we have for the new dose?" --
new programs in the private sector, but there is still not

gapfill.text: Two families again. Things you count one by one -- patients, programs, doctors -- take HOW MANY, and A FEW or SEVERAL. Things you cannot count one by one -- data, evidence, access, funding, coverage -- take HOW MUCH, and A LITTLE. And to compare the past with today, USED TO does the work: "There used to be very little access; now there is more."

lf.title: Can you count it, or not?
items: evidence do we have for the new dose?
new programs in the private sector.
funding in the public sector yet.

lf.text: A and C count things one by one: one patient, two patients; one program, two programs. They take many and several. B and D cannot be counted one by one: you never say "two evidences" or "three fundings". They take much and a little. In Portuguese, "muito" is one word for both -- and that single difference is worth five minutes of your life.

bank.label: Useful language for a data update
items: Here is the market access update for Brazil.
The patient population is large, and it is growing.
We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose.
There are several new programs in the private sector.
There is not much public funding yet.
Two years ago there used to be almost no access.

items.situation: Marc opens the review: "So, how is access going?" Give him the headline in one sentence.
tips: Here is the market access update for Brazil. The patient population is large, and it is growing.
Access is better than last year. So far, so good.

items.situation: "How many patients can take this treatment?"
tips: The patient population is large, and it is growing in several regions.
We have a lot of patients who can take it -- the population is large.

items.situation: "How much evidence do we have for the higher dose?"
tips: We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose.
There is a lot of new evidence, and more data every quarter.

items.situation: He asks about the public sector. Report the problem honestly, without apologizing.
tips: There is not much funding in the public sector yet, and the decision is slow.
We have a few programs under review, but there is very little public funding.

items.situation: Compare today with two years ago. Use the chunk of the lesson.
tips: Two years ago there used to be almost no access. Today there is much more.
There used to be very little evidence in Portuguese; now we have a lot.

items.situation: You want to know if he has news from Paris before the call ends. Ask him.
tips: Are there any updates on the reimbursement from your side?
Do you have any news from Paris about the funding?
